CLARITY.justification: Based on the provided summary, the paper is well-organized with a clear method->experiment->conclusion structure and states its main contributions early (introduction of cross-correlation Gram terms). Notation and methodology are described consistently (use of transformed Gram matrices, VGG-19 layers, Lcc addition), and examples/applications (synthesis, inpainting, season transfer) appear informative. Writing quality is reported as good and related work coverage adequate. Minor presentation issues remain: discussion of the important hyperparameter choices (translation offsets δ) and runtime overhead could be made clearer or better justified/automated, preventing a top score.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper states its main claims clearly and supports them with experiments against appropriate baselines (Gatys et al. and patch-based methods), with code and hyperparameters reported. The proposed transformed-Gram formulation and cross-correlation loss are mathematically straightforward and appear correct. However, the experimental methodology lacks reported quantitative evaluation metrics and statistical tests, and the method depends on hand-chosen translation offsets (δ), a limitation the authors acknowledge. Overall the results and limitations reported justify the conclusions about improvements on regular and symmetric textures, so only minor technical issues remain.

ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a concrete, novel modification to Gram-based CNN texture representations by computing Gram matrices between feature maps and spatially transformed (translated/flipped) feature maps and adding a cross-correlation loss Lcc — a clear extension beyond Gatys et al. While texture synthesis/style transfer is a well-studied problem, the work clearly differentiates its contribution and non-trivially encodes long-range spatial structure and symmetry missing from standard Gram statistics. The approach is nonetheless closely related to existing Gram-based methods and has practical limitations (manual offset selection, increased runtime), so it is notably novel but not a radical departure.

main_claims: Introduce a modification to Gram-based CNN texture representations that encodes spatial co-occurrences by computing Gram matrices between feature maps and spatially transformed feature maps (e.g. translations, flips).

The proposed cross-correlation loss (Lcc) captures long-range spatial structure and global symmetry properties in generated textures, improving rendering of regular and symmetric textures compared to the original Gram-based method (Gatys et al.).
The method enables applications beyond texture synthesis, specifically texture inpainting and season (style) transfer, producing visually improved consistency with the non-missing image regions.
_summary.methodology: Compute transformed Gram matrices by taking inner-products between feature maps and spatially transformed versions (translations and flips) of feature maps from selected VGG-19 layers, add their mean-squared error as a cross-correlation loss Lcc to the original style loss, and optimize the input image to match both standard and transformed Gram statistics.
baselines: Gatys et al. (2015a,b) Gram-based texture/style synthesis
limitations: The choice of translation offsets (δ) is important, depends on layer and reference texture, and is difficult to select automatically.
Adding cross-correlation terms increases generation time by roughly 80% in the authors' experiments.
The method does not necessarily provide benefits for unstructured reference textures.
Transformed Gramians as presented do not consider diagonal offsets.
_summary.topic_area: Computer Vision - texture synthesis / image generation / style transfer
